tweets.title: INDIAN NAVY TO COMMISSION STEALTH FRIGATE INS MAHENDRAGIRI The Indian Navy is set to commission INS Mahendragiri (F38), its sixth indigenous Project 17A stealth frigate, at Visakhapatnam on 11 July 2026. Key Features of INS Mahendragiri: - Stealth Technology: A special design that makes the ship hard to spot on enemy radar. - Heavy Firepower: Fitted with surface-to-air and surface-to-surface missiles to attack enemy ships, planes, and land targets. - Anti-Submarine Defense: Includes advanced systems to detect and fight underwater threats. - Design: Crafted by the Navy's own Warship Design Bureau. Named after the Mahendragiri mountain range in the Eastern Ghats, the warship was designed by the Indian Navy’s Warship Design Bureau (WDB) and built by Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders Limited (MDL) in Mumbai.
tweets.content:
INDIAN NAVY TO COMMISSION STEALTH FRIGATE INS MAHENDRAGIRI

The Indian Navy is set to commission INS Mahendragiri (F38), its sixth indigenous Project 17A stealth frigate, at Visakhapatnam on 11 July 2026.

Key Features of INS Mahendragiri:

- Stealth Technology: A special design that makes the ship hard to spot on enemy radar.
- Heavy Firepower: Fitted with surface-to-air and surface-to-surface missiles to attack enemy ships, planes, and land targets.
- Anti-Submarine Defense: Includes advanced systems to detect and fight underwater threats.
- Design: Crafted by the Navy's own Warship Design Bureau.

Named after the Mahendragiri mountain range in the Eastern Ghats, the warship was designed by the Indian Navy’s Warship Design Bureau (WDB) and built by Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders Limited (MDL) in Mumbai.

tweets.title: DIRCM is an important capability if an aircraft is expected to operate in environments where shoulder-fired MANPADS are a credible threat. It can also improve survivability against short-range IR-guided air-to-air missiles, although its effectiveness against modern imaging infrared (IIR) seekers is still not well understood in the public domain / attributable data. One design trade-off rarely discussed is RCS. Those large spherical DIRCM turrets are external protrusions, so it is worth asking whether they introduce a measurable radar cross-section penalty. The real question isn’t whether there is any increase, but whether the survivability gained against IR threats far outweighs any marginal increase in RCS. That’s an interesting engineering trade-off.
tweets.content:
DIRCM is an important capability if an aircraft is expected to operate in environments where shoulder-fired MANPADS are a credible threat. It can also improve survivability against short-range IR-guided air-to-air missiles, although its effectiveness against modern imaging infrared (IIR) seekers is still not well understood in the public domain / attributable data.

One design trade-off rarely discussed is RCS. Those large spherical DIRCM turrets are external protrusions, so it is worth asking whether they introduce a measurable radar cross-section penalty.

The real question isn’t whether there is any increase, but whether the survivability gained against IR threats far outweighs any marginal increase in RCS. That’s an interesting engineering trade-off.

tweets.title: I understand every argument in favour of Rafale, and I agree that India needs Rafale today, if not yesterday. But here’s the question I’m trying to answer. If the Government signs a deal for 94 Rafales to be manufactured in India, and deliveries begin in 2032, then even at a production rate of around 12 aircraft per year, the fleet would grow roughly like this: 2032: Deliveries begin 2033: 13 aircraft inducted 2034: 25 2035: 37 2036: 49 2037: 61 2038: 73 2039: 85 2040: 94 How does this solve the IAF’s immediate squadron shortage? The real question isn’t whether Rafale is an excellent aircraft.. it is. The question is: what is the bridge between now and 2032–2040? and this is F4 (not F5) P.S. I’ve deliberately simplified the induction timeline by assuming a near-steady production rate. In reality, there would likely be a production ramp-up in the initial years and a ramp-down towards the end, so the exact yearly numbers would differ. The point is to illustrate the overall timeline, not predict the precise delivery schedule.
tweets.content:
I understand every argument in favour of Rafale, and I agree that India needs Rafale today, if not yesterday.

But here’s the question I’m trying to answer.

If the Government signs a deal for 94 Rafales to be manufactured in India, and deliveries begin in 2032, then even at a production rate of around 12 aircraft per year, the fleet would grow roughly like this:

2032: Deliveries begin
2033: 13 aircraft inducted
2034: 25
2035: 37
2036: 49
2037: 61
2038: 73
2039: 85
2040: 94

How does this solve the IAF’s immediate squadron shortage?

The real question isn’t whether Rafale is an excellent aircraft.. it is. The question is: what is the bridge between now and 2032–2040? and this is F4 (not F5)

P.S. I’ve deliberately simplified the induction timeline by assuming a near-steady production rate. In reality, there would likely be a production ramp-up in the initial years and a ramp-down towards the end, so the exact yearly numbers would differ. The point is to illustrate the overall timeline, not predict the precise delivery schedule.

tweets.content:
BREAKING: ‘Anti-National’ Content Found in J&K School Library Books

The Jammu & Kashmir administration has suspended eight School Education Department officials, blacklisted the authors and publishers, and withdrawn two books from government school libraries after they were found containing content described by officials as "highly inappropriate."

The books referred to Jammu & Kashmir as "India-occupied Kashmir" and "Indian-held Kashmir", and described Terrorist Maqbool Bhat as "Shaheed."

Authorities have ordered a high-level inquiry, while copies already distributed to schools have been withdrawn.

tweets.title: My two cents on rocket force debate : Most commentators from the IAF are naturally looking at this through an Air Force lens, and that’s fair. Their concerns about duplication, joint planning, and command structures are valid. But I think one important question is often missed. If India’s ambition is to eventually field thousands of conventional ballistic, cruise, and hypersonic missiles, can missile warfare continue to be just another responsibility within the IAF, competing with fighters, AWACS, tankers, transport aircraft, and air defence for budgets and attention? At that scale, the debate changes. It isnt about IAF vs Rocket Force instead its question of whether missile warfare has become important enough to deserve its own doctrine, procurement priorities, and long-term institutional focus.
tweets.content:
My two cents on rocket force debate :

Most commentators from the IAF are naturally looking at this through an Air Force lens, and that’s fair. Their concerns about duplication, joint planning, and command structures are valid.

But I think one important question is often missed.

If India’s ambition is to eventually field thousands of conventional ballistic, cruise, and hypersonic missiles, can missile warfare continue to be just another responsibility within the IAF, competing with fighters, AWACS, tankers, transport aircraft, and air defence for budgets and attention?

At that scale, the debate changes.

It isnt about IAF vs Rocket Force instead its question of whether missile warfare has become important enough to deserve its own doctrine, procurement priorities, and long-term institutional focus.

tweets.title: Yes, no country can eliminate imports overnight. But every import should hurt our self-respect. We should feel ashamed that we still depend on others for critical technologies, and let that discomfort drive us to build them ourselves. The goal should be simple: zero critical imports and as PM says an Atmanirbhar India. Self-respect creates ambition. Ambition creates capability. That sense of self-respect must trickle from the new generation of leaders, who refuse to accept dependence as normal, to the older generation that grew up believing it was inevitable.
tweets.content:
Yes, no country can eliminate imports overnight. But every import should hurt our self-respect. We should feel ashamed that we still depend on others for critical technologies, and let that discomfort drive us to build them ourselves.

The goal should be simple: zero critical imports and as PM says an Atmanirbhar India.

Self-respect creates ambition. Ambition creates capability. That sense of self-respect must trickle from the new generation of leaders, who refuse to accept dependence as normal, to the older generation that grew up believing it was inevitable.

Defence Matrix (@Defencematrix1)

For country like India, Imports can't stop overnight since we were world's largest arms Importer for many decades, but a transition should come, where a roadmap is needed on where and how Imports should be done, Clarity on Imports should be there and companies which are actually trying to develop something locally on their own should also get rewarded. Screwdrivergiri also can't stop overnight but there should also be a clarity on the real nature of manufacturing. White labelling should be a crime till the system is completely localised, even in that case, till it is evolved with R&D for local requirement. Domestic name should not be given till it is domesticated and the issue is not just with private sector, it's equally with PSUs, in-fact PSUs survived on the very same thing, so taking this from private sector would be wrong, but the method should be corrected, hope this online discussion and debates should help in this cause.
